summary_quote: Decision: the pipeline has no Experiment boundary that owns seed, split, and metric capture; aims-stated reproducibility and no-leakage commitments are structurally unreachable without one. Recommend extracting an Experiment seam (Splits + Seeder + MetricsSink) before the next training run is treated as a result.

findings.title: No Experiment boundary owns seed, split, and metric capture — the three commitments the aims make are scattered as missing concerns across train.py and data.py
findings.explanation: Context: the aims commit this project to three properties — reproducibility (identical loss curves under same config), a train/val/test split with no leakage, and per-epoch metrics culminating in a single test-set accuracy number. The current structure has four functions (load_config, load_full_dataset, make_model, train) wired together inline in train.py:43-66. Decision (observed): the PR places all three commitments as omissions in the same monolithic train() body — no seeding before DataLoader construction at line 51, no split before the loader sees the dataset at line 50, no metric sink beyond print() at line 61. There is no module that owns the experiment lifecycle (seed -> split -> train epoch -> val epoch -> test eval -> persist metrics). The team-data-ml-reviewer correctly blocked on each individual omission; the structural reading is that the omissions cluster because the seam they would attach to does not exist. Consequences: good — the current shape is short and readable for a spike. Bad — every fix the Stage 2 reviewer recommends has to be threaded into the same 25-line train() function with no place to hang. Recommendation: approve with revisions. Extract an Experiment seam — seed_everything(seed), make_splits(dataset, seed), and a MetricsSink (even a JSON-lines writer) that train() calls per epoch.
findings.suggestion: Restructure src/train.py so train(cfg) reads: (1) seed_everything(cfg['seed']) first; (2) dataset = load_full_dataset() then train_ds, val_ds, test_ds = make_splits(dataset, ratios=cfg['split'], seed=cfg['seed']); (3) a MetricsSink (JsonlMetricsSink) that train() writes {epoch, train_loss, val_loss, val_acc} to; (4) a final evaluate(model, test_ds) call that writes one {test_acc} line. Add seed: 42 and split: [0.7, 0.15, 0.15] and run_dir: runs/baseline to configs/default.yaml. The four extractions together are ~60 lines and make every Stage 2 high finding fixable in the seam where it belongs.

findings.title: Config is consumed as a raw dict via magic string keys at every callsite — the schema lives implicitly in default.yaml and the team will discover drift at runtime
findings.explanation: Context: this is a research pipeline used for comparing runs. Decision (observed): load_config returns dict and every reader downstream uses string subscripts. The team-security-reviewer's yaml.safe_load with no schema validation finding is a symptom of the same decision: there is no typed config object, so neither the security boundary nor the call sites have anything to validate against. Forecloses the cheap path to comparing runs by diffing configs — without a typed schema, configs from six weeks ago will silently omit fields that were added later and you won't know which value was used at train time. Recommendation: approve with revisions. Introduce a Config dataclass in src/config.py; have load_config parse YAML into it; let every downstream function take Config rather than dict.
findings.suggestion: Create src/config.py with a Config dataclass: @dataclass(frozen=True) class Config: seed: int; batch_size: int; epochs: int; learning_rate: float; hidden_dim: int; input_dim: int; output_dim: int; split: tuple[float, float, float]; run_dir: Path. Change load_config(path) -> Config to parse YAML and return the dataclass. Update make_model(cfg: Config), train(cfg: Config), and make_splits(cfg: Config, dataset) to consume the typed object. This single change resolves the security reviewer's schema-validation finding, the python-reviewer's load_config return-type finding, and the readability-engineer's magic config keys finding as a side effect.

stage_handoff_notes: For the lead-project-manager: the aim alignment here is partial in a structurally meaningful way. The aims explicitly commit to reproducibility, no-leakage splits, per-epoch metrics, and a final test accuracy — the team-data-ml-reviewer's block is the right call against the aim, not against the code style. My architectural reading is that the PR is not just missing four features; it lacks the seam (an Experiment boundary) where those features would attach. For the Aggregator: the headline ADR is the missing Experiment boundary (finding 1). All five Stage 2 team-data-ml findings, the team-performance num_workers / dataset-regen findings, and the team-security YAML-schema finding cluster at this seam — they are not independent issues. The second finding (typed config) explains why three peer-stage findings (load_config return type, magic keys, no YAML schema validation) all surface together. Quoting the structural ADR collapses ~8 lower-stage findings into 2 architectural ones without losing information.
